```python
    X, y, test_size=0.20, random_state=42, stratify=y
)
print("Train size:", X_train.shape, " Test size:", X_test.shape)

# Step 5: Initialize the Random Forest Classifier
rf_clf = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=100,
    max_features='sqrt',
    criterion='gini',
    random_state=42,
    oob_score=True,
    n_jobs=-1
)

# Step 6: Train the model
rf_clf.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Step 7: OOB Score (built-in validation)
print("OOB Score:", rf_clf.oob_score_)

# Step 8: Predict on test data
y_pred = rf_clf.predict(X_test)

# Step 9: Confusion Matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)
print("Confusion Matrix:")
print(cm)

# Step 10: Classification Report
print("Classification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=iris.target_names))

# Step 11: Test Accuracy
print("Test Accuracy:", accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred))

# Step 12: Feature Importance Plot (Top 5)
importances = rf_clf.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)[::-1][:5]

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.bar(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color='steelblue', edgecolor='black')
plt.xticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices], rotation=45)
plt.ylabel("Feature Importance (Gini)")
plt.title("Top-5 Feature Importances — Random Forest (Iris)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("rf_feature_importance.png", dpi=120)
plt.show()
```

**Expected Output (representative):**
```
OOB Score: 0.95
Confusion Matrix:
[[10  0  0]
 [ 0 10  0]
 [ 0  0 10]]
              precision    recall  f1-score   support
      setosa       1.00      1.00      1.00        10
  versicolor       1.00      1.00      1.00        10
   virginica       1.00      1.00      1.00        10
Test Accuracy: 1.0
```

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** `Part B / 14 Marks`
> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **Gini Impurity** and **Entropy** as split criteria for decision trees. Compute both for a node containing 50 positives and 50 negatives. Which criterion would scikit-learn's `RandomForestClassifier` use by default? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare a **Random Forest** with a **single Decision Tree** for the **Breast Cancer Wisconsin dataset**. Report **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score** for both models and comment on the results using a 5-fold cross-validation. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Gini Impurity:** Measures the probability of **misclassifying** a randomly chosen instance if it were labeled according to the class distribution of the node.

$$G(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$

**Entropy:** Measures the **information disorder** (in bits) of the node.

$$H(t) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

**Computation for a 50/50 Node:**

$$
p_+ = 0.5, \quad p_- = 0.5
$$

**Gini:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= 1 - (0.5^2 + 0.5^2) \\
  &= 1 - 0.50 \\
  &= 0.500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Entropy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -\left(0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.5 \log_2 0.5\right) \\
  &= -(0.5 \times (-1) + 0.5 \times (-1)) \\
  &= 1.000 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Default in scikit-learn:** `criterion='gini'` for `RandomForestClassifier`.

### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

```python
# ============================================================
# Comparison: Random Forest vs Single Decision Tree
# Dataset: Breast Cancer Wisconsin
# ============================================================

from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import make_scorer, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score
import numpy as np

# Load data
data = load_breast_cancer()
X, y = data.data, data.target

# Initialize models
dt = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)

# Stratified 5-Fold CV
cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

# Evaluate both models
for name, model in [("Decision Tree", dt), ("Random Forest", rf)]:
    acc  = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring='accuracy').mean()
    prec = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring='precision').mean()
    rec  = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring='recall').mean()
    f1   = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring='f1').mean()
    print(f"--- {name} ---")
    print(f"Accuracy : {acc:.4f}")
    print(f"Precision: {prec:.4f}")
    print(f"Recall   : {rec:.4f}")
    print(f"F1-Score : {f1:.4f}\n")
```

**Expected Observation (representative):**
| Metric | Decision Tree | Random Forest |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ~0.93 | ~0.96 |
| Precision | ~0.93 | ~0.96 |
| Recall | ~0.95 | ~0.97 |
| F1-Score | ~0.94 | ~0.96 |

**Comment:** The Random Forest **consistently outperforms** the single Decision Tree across all metrics due to **variance reduction** through ensemble averaging. The improvement is most pronounced in **recall** and **F1-score**, indicating fewer missed malignant cases — a critical factor in medical diagnosis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Random Forest Lab Exam**

- ✅ Random Forest = **Bagging + Random Feature Subset** + **Majority Voting**.
- ✅ Use `sklearn.ensemble.RandomForestClassifier` for classification tasks.
- ✅ Two randomization sources: **row sampling (bootstrap)** and **column sampling (`max_features`)**.
- ✅ Default `max_features` for classification = `sqrt(M)`; for regression = `M/3`.
- ✅ Default split criterion = **`gini`**; alternative = **`entropy`**.
- ✅ Bootstrap sample contains ~**63.2%** unique instances; rest form **OOB set**.
- ✅ OOB score provides **free internal cross-validation**; enable with `oob_score=True`.
- ✅ **Hyperparameters to tune**: `n_estimators`, `max_depth`, `max_features`, `min_samples_split`, `min_samples_leaf`.
- ✅ Always set `random_state` for **reproducibility** in lab records.
- ✅ Evaluation metrics: **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score** + **Confusion Matrix**.
- ✅ Use `cross_val_score` with `StratifiedKFold` for **balanced** cross-validation.
- ✅ `feature_importances_` attribute reveals **Gini-based importance** of each feature.
- ✅ Random Forest **reduces variance** (not bias) compared to single decision tree.
- ✅ Always **compare with a single decision tree** — KTU exam expects this comparison.
- ✅ For multi-class: use `classification_report` with `target_names` for readability.
- ✅ **Gini formula**: $G = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ | **Entropy formula**: $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$.
- ✅ Trees are grown to **maximum depth** (no pruning) — regularization is via ensemble averaging.
- ✅ Real-world analogy: "**Board of medical experts**" voting on a diagnosis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Train and evaluate a boosted model using AdaBoost Classifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL408 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module List of Experiments
> - **Topic:** Train and evaluate a boosted model using AdaBoost Classifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting)** is a powerful ensemble learning meta-algorithm formally introduced by *Yoav Freund and Robert Schapire in 1995-1996*. It sequentially combines a fleet of *weak learners* (typically shallow decision stumps) into a single, high-variance-corrected *strong learner* by adaptively re-weighting misclassified training instances at every iteration.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus vocabulary:
> **AdaBoostClassifier** is a Boosting-family ensemble estimator in `sklearn.ensemble` that fits a base classifier (default = `DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1)`) on repeatedly modified versions of the dataset. The modifications take the form of applying weights $w_i$ to each training sample, where weights are increased for previously misclassified samples and decreased for correctly classified ones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Boosting is an *ensemble technique* where models are trained **sequentially** (not in parallel like Bagging/Random Forest). Each successive model attempts to correct the errors of its predecessor by focusing computational "attention" on the previously misclassified, high-weight samples.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iterative Tutor"

Imagine you are a **student preparing for a difficult exam** by repeatedly solving a problem set:

- **Iteration 1:** You attempt all questions. You get some wrong.
- **Tutor's Action (AdaBoost's weight update):** The tutor **circles your wrong answers in red** and tells you: *"Focus 3x more time on these red-marked problems tomorrow."*
- **Iteration 2:** You re-attempt the set, but now you spend extra effort on the previously wrong questions. You solve a few more, but make new mistakes elsewhere.
- **Tutor's Action:** The tutor again **highlights the new mistakes in red** and tells you to focus on those *plus* the previous unsolved ones.
- **Final Model:** Your final exam answer = a **weighted combination of all your past attempts**, where answers from iterations where you performed well are trusted more (higher $\alpha_t$).

This is exactly how AdaBoost works — the **data points themselves** are the "questions", the **base learners (stumps)** are the "iterative attempts", and the **alpha weights** are the "trust scores" assigned to each attempt.

## 1.3 Why AdaBoost is Revolutionary

| Property | Bagging (e.g., Random Forest) | **AdaBoost** |
|---|---|---|
| Training Mode | Parallel | **Sequential** |
| Sample Weighting | Uniform bootstrap sampling | **Adaptive, instance-wise** |
| Goal | Reduce **Variance** | Reduce **Bias** (primarily) |
| Weak Learner | Full-depth tree | **Decision Stump** (1-level tree) |
| Final Combination | Average / Majority Vote | **Weighted Sum with Signs** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Fact:** AdaBoost is famously *adaptive* because it adjusts to the *errors* of the weak hypotheses observed in previous rounds. This is the precise reason for the prefix "Ada-".

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Blueprint

The AdaBoost algorithm (binary classification, $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$) executes the following deterministic pipeline:

### **Phase A — Initialization (Iteration $t = 1$)**

Every training instance begins with an *equal* importance weight. For a dataset of $N$ samples:

$$w_i^{(1)} = \frac{1}{N}, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$$

This initialization step is critical because it expresses the *prior assumption* that no sample is more important than another before the first weak learner has spoken.

### **Phase B — Iterative Learning Loop (for $t = 1$ to $T$)**

At each iteration $t$, a weak classifier $h_t : \mathcal{X} \to \{-1, +1\}$ is trained using the *current* weight distribution $W^{(t)}$.

**Step B1 — Compute Weighted Error Rate ($\epsilon_t$):**
The error is *not* a simple misclassification count. It is the **sum of weights** of the samples that were misclassified. This forces the algorithm to "feel" the cumulative penalty of high-weight mistakes.

$$\epsilon_t = \sum_{i : h_t(x_i) \neq y_i} w_i^{(t)}$$

**Step B2 — Compute the Learner's Trust Score ($\alpha_t$):**
A learner that is only slightly better than random guessing ($\epsilon_t \approx 0.5$) receives a **near-zero $\alpha_t$** (it is essentially ignored). A learner with very low error ($\epsilon_t \to 0$) receives a **very high $\alpha_t$** (it is trusted heavily).

$$\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln \left( \frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t} \right)$$

**Step B3 — Update Sample Weights:**
The vector of sample weights is updated multiplicatively. Misclassified samples have their weight **multiplied by $e^{\alpha_t}$** (boosted), while correctly classified samples have their weight **multiplied by $e^{-\alpha_t}$** (demoted).

$$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\left( -\alpha_t \cdot y_i \cdot h_t(x_i) \right)$$

After updating, weights are **normalized** so that $\sum_i w_i^{(t+1)} = 1$.

### **Phase C — Final Strong Hypothesis**

The ensemble output is a **sign-weighted majority vote** across all $T$ weak learners. The sign of the weighted sum determines the predicted class.

$$H_{final}(x) = \text{sign} \left( \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \, h_t(x) \right)$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why divide by $2$ in $\alpha_t$?** The factor $1/2$ arises directly from the **exponential loss minimization** derivation. AdaBoost can be proven to be a coordinate-descent optimization of the exponential loss function $L = \sum_i \exp(-y_i F(x_i))$.
- **Why exponential weighting?** The exponential function is chosen because its gradient (with respect to the margin $y_i F(x_i)$) is itself an exponential. This means **large margin points decay super-linearly**, providing AdaBoost with its empirically observed **margin-maximization property**.
- **Why normalize weights?** Normalization ensures the weight distribution remains a valid probability distribution that can be sampled from (or used as a weighted loss) in the next iteration.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial Weight | $w_i^{(1)} = 1/N$ | Uniform prior belief |
| 2 | Weighted Error | $\epsilon_t = \sum_{i: h_t(x_i) \neq y_i} w_i^{(t)}$ | Quantifies learner quality |
| 3 | Trust Score (Learner Weight) | $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2} \ln \!\left( \tfrac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t} \right)$ | Voting power of learner $t$ |
| 4 | Sample Weight Update | $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$ | Focus on hard samples |
| 5 | Normalization | $Z_t = \sum_i w_i^{(t+1)}; \;\; w_i^{(t+1)} \leftarrow w_i^{(t+1)} / Z_t$ | Maintains probability distribution |
| 6 | Strong Classifier | $H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x) \right)$ | Final ensemble prediction |
| 7 | Exponential Loss | $L_{\text{exp}} = \sum_i \exp(-y_i F(x_i))$ | Theoretical upper bound minimized by AdaBoost |
| 8 | Margin | $\text{margin}(x,y) = y F(x) / \sum_t \vert \alpha_t \vert$ | Confidence of classification |
| 9 | sklearn Parameter `n_estimators` | $T$ | Number of boosting rounds |
| 10 | sklearn Parameter `learning_rate` | $\nu$ | Shrinks $\alpha_t$ contribution: $\alpha_t \leftarrow \nu \cdot \alpha_t$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constraint for the Algorithm to Work:** $\epsilon_t < 0.5$ at every iteration. If $\epsilon_t \ge 0.5$, the term $(1 - \epsilon_t)/\epsilon_t \le 1$, making $\alpha_t \le 0$, which **breaks the algorithm**. In practice, sklearn automatically re-initializes weights if this occurs.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Face Detection:** The original *Viola-Jones face detector* (2001) used an AdaBoost cascade — a foundational algorithm in computer vision and smartphone cameras.
- **Credit Card Fraud Detection:** Banks use AdaBoost over decision stumps for real-time transaction classification.
- **Bioinformatics:** Cancer subtype classification from gene expression data.
- **Spam Filtering:** Email spam classifiers historically employed AdaBoost on n-gram features.
- **Customer Churn Prediction:** Telecom and SaaS companies use AdaBoost to predict which subscribers will cancel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of the Weight Update Rule

### **Objective:** Derive the optimal $\alpha_t$ that minimizes the exponential loss.

Consider the exponential loss after adding the $t$-th weak learner $h_t$ to the strong classifier $F_{t-1}$:

$$F_t(x) = F_{t-1}(x) + \alpha_t h_t(x)$$

The total loss is:

$$L_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\left( -y_i F_t(x_i) \right) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\left( -y_i F_{t-1}(x_i) \right) \cdot \exp\left( -\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i) \right)$$

Define the shorthand $w_i' = \exp(-y_i F_{t-1}(x_i))$. Then:

$$L_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i' \cdot \exp\left( -\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i) \right)$$

Because $y_i h_t(x_i) \in \{-1, +1\}$, we split the sum into **correctly classified** ($C$) and **misclassified** ($M$) subsets:

$$L_t = e^{-\alpha_t} \sum_{i \in C} w_i' \; + \; e^{\alpha_t} \sum_{i \in M} w_i'$$

Let the weighted error rate on misclassified samples be $\epsilon = \sum_{i \in M} w_i'$, and correspondingly the correct weight is $1 - \epsilon = \sum_{i \in C} w_i'$ (assuming normalized weights). Substituting:

$$L_t = (1 - \epsilon) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon \, e^{\alpha_t}$$

### **Step 3.1.1 — Take the derivative with respect to $\alpha_t$**

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \alpha_t} = -(1 - \epsilon) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon \, e^{\alpha_t}$$

### **Step 3.1.2 — Set the derivative to zero to find the minimum**

$$-(1 - \epsilon) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon \, e^{\alpha_t} = 0$$

$$\epsilon \, e^{\alpha_t} = (1 - \epsilon) e^{-\alpha_t}$$

### **Step 3.1.3 — Solve for $\alpha_t$ algebraically**

$$\frac{\epsilon}{1 - \epsilon} = \frac{e^{-\alpha_t}}{e^{\alpha_t}} = e^{-2\alpha_t}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \left( \frac{\epsilon}{1 - \epsilon} \right) = -2\alpha_t$$

Therefore, the **optimal $\alpha_t$** that minimizes the exponential loss is:

$$\boxed{\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln \left( \frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t} \right)}$$

This precisely matches the algorithmic formula in the formula sheet. The derivation confirms that AdaBoost is **provably optimal** under the exponential loss function — a beautiful connection between algorithmic heuristics and formal convex optimization.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Manual AdaBoost on a Toy Dataset

Consider a 1-D classification problem with $N = 5$ samples:

| Sample $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | +1 |
| 2 | 2 | +1 |
| 3 | 3 | -1 |
| 4 | 4 | -1 |
| 5 | 5 | +1 |

### **Initialization**

$$w_i^{(1)} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \quad \forall i$$

### **Iteration 1 ($t = 1$)**

Suppose the best decision stump is $h_1(x) = +1$ if $x < 2.5$ else $-1$.

**Predictions vs Ground Truth:**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $h_1(x_i)$ | Status |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | +1 | +1 | ✅ Correct |
| 2 | 2 | +1 | +1 | ✅ Correct |
| 3 | 3 | -1 | -1 | ✅ Correct |
| 4 | 4 | -1 | -1 | ✅ Correct |
| 5 | 5 | +1 | -1 | ❌ Misclassified |

**Compute Weighted Error:**

$$\epsilon_1 = w_5^{(1)} = 0.2$$

**Compute Trust Score $\alpha_1$:**

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{1}{2} \ln \left( \frac{1 - 0.2}{0.2} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(4) = \frac{1}{2}(1.3863) \approx 0.6931$$

**Update Weights:**

For the misclassified sample ($i = 5$): $w_5^{(2)} = 0.2 \cdot e^{0.6931} = 0.2 \cdot 2.0 = 0.4$

For all correctly classified samples: $w_i^{(2)} = 0.2 \cdot e^{-0.6931} = 0.2 \cdot 0.5 = 0.1$

**Normalize:** Sum $= 4(0.1) + 0.4 = 0.8$. New normalized weights: $w_i^{(2)} = 0.125$ for $i \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ and $w_5^{(2)} = 0.5$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The misclassified sample $x_5$ now carries **50% of the total weight** — a massive amplification from the original 20%. The next weak learner will be **forced to pay enormous attention** to sample 5.

### **Iteration 2 ($t = 2$)**

The new decision stump (biased towards $x_5$) might be $h_2(x) = +1$ if $x \ge 4.5$ else $-1$. This corrects sample 5 at the cost of misclassifying samples 1, 2, 3, 4 (but their weights are now tiny, so the weighted error is acceptable).

This iterative, weight-amplification process continues until $T$ estimators are built, after which the final classifier is the **sign of the weighted sum** of all stump predictions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hyperparameter Impact Table

| Hyperparameter | Effect of Increasing | Risk of Excessive Value |
|---|---|---|
| `n_estimators` ($T$) | Higher training accuracy; finer decision boundary | **Overfitting** on noisy data; longer training time |
| `learning_rate` ($\nu$) | More aggressive correction per round | Overfitting; can make $\alpha_t$ dominant and destabilize |
| `base_estimator` depth | Captures more complex patterns | Defeats the "weak learner" philosophy; risk of overfitting |
| `algorithm` ('SAMME'/'SAMME.R') | SAMME.R uses class probabilities, often faster | May be sensitive to noisy probabilities |

> [!WARNING]
> **The `n_estimators` vs `learning_rate` Trade-off (KTU Pitfall):** There is an inverse relationship. If you halve `learning_rate`, you should typically **double `n_estimators`** to maintain similar model capacity. Forgetting this trade-off is a common error in lab examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AdaBoost Sequential Ensemble Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Dataset X and Labels y] --> B[Initialize Sample Weights w equals 1 over N]
    B --> C[Iteration t equals 1]
    C --> D[Train Weak Learner h sub t on weighted data]
    D --> E[Compute Weighted Error epsilon sub t]
    E --> F{epsilon sub t less than 0.5}
    F -- No --> G[Re-initialize or Stop]
    F -- Yes --> H[Compute alpha sub t = 0.5 ln of 1 minus epsilon over epsilon]
    H --> I[Update Sample Weights w sub t plus 1]
    I --> J[Normalize Weights so sum equals 1]
    J --> K{t less than T estimators}
    K -- Yes --> C
    K -- No --> L[Combine all h sub t weighted by alpha sub t]
    L --> M[Final H of x = sign of sum alpha sub t times h sub t of x]
    M --> N[Output Predictions]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — AdaBoost in a Typical ML Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Data CSV] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Handle Missing Values]
    B --> B2[Encode Categorical Variables]
    B --> B3[Feature Scaling]
    B1 --> C[Train Test Split 80 20]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Instantiate AdaBoostClassifier]
    D --> D1[base estimator = DecisionTreeClassifier max depth 1]
    D --> D2[n estimators = 50]
    D --> D3[learning rate = 1.0]
    D --> D4[random state = 42]
    D1 --> E[model fit X train y train]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Sequential Boosting Loop]
    F --> F1[Round 1 Sample Stump and Update Weights]
    F --> F2[Round 2 Sample Stump and Update Weights]
    F --> F3[Round N Sample Stump and Update Weights]
    F1 --> G[Strong Classifier H of x]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[model predict X test]
    H --> I[Evaluation Metrics]
    I --> I1[Accuracy Score]
    I --> I2[Confusion Matrix]
    I --> I3[Classification Report]
    I --> I4[ROC AUC Score]
```

## 4.3 Weight Re-Distribution Mechanism (Misclassified Sample Focus)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample i has weight w sub t] --> B{Classifier h sub t correct}
    B -- Yes --> C[New weight = w sub t times exp minus alpha sub t]
    B -- No --> D[New weight = w sub t times exp plus alpha sub t]
    C --> E[Weight Decreased by factor 2]
    D --> F[Weight Increased by factor 2]
    E --> G[Normalize all weights]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Distribution ready for Iteration t plus 1]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define the AdaBoost algorithm. State the formula used to compute the weight ($\alpha_t$) assigned to each weak learner at iteration $t$, and explain the significance of this weight.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting) is an **ensemble learning meta-algorithm** that sequentially combines multiple *weak learners* (typically decision stumps) to form a single *strong learner*. At each iteration, misclassified training samples have their weights increased, forcing subsequent learners to focus on the harder examples.

The weight $\alpha_t$ assigned to the $t$-th weak learner is computed as:

$$\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln \left( \frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t} \right)$$

where $\epsilon_t$ is the **weighted classification error** of the $t$-th learner on the training set.

**Significance:**
- $\alpha_t$ quantifies the **trustworthiness** (or voting power) of the $t$-th learner in the final ensemble.
- Learners with low error $\epsilon_t$ receive **high $\alpha_t$** values, meaning their predictions dominate the final vote.
- Learners with error $\epsilon_t \to 0.5$ (barely better than random) receive $\alpha_t \to 0$ and are effectively ignored.
- This mechanism ensures that only competent weak learners contribute meaningfully to the final classifier.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for significance)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]**
*How are the training sample weights updated in each iteration of AdaBoost? Why is normalization of weights necessary after each update?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The weight update rule for each training sample $i$ at iteration $t$ is:

$$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\left( -\alpha_t \cdot y_i \cdot h_t(x_i) \right)$$

Since $y_i h_t(x_i) = +1$ for correctly classified samples and $-1$ for misclassified samples, this rule **multiplies the weight of misclassified samples by $e^{\alpha_t}$** (amplification) and the weight of correctly classified samples by $e^{-\alpha_t}$ (demotion).

**Why normalization is necessary:**
- After updating, the sum $\sum_i w_i^{(t+1)}$ is no longer equal to 1.
- Normalization (dividing each weight by the total sum) ensures that the weights form a **valid probability distribution**, i.e., $\sum_i w_i^{(t+1)} = 1$.
- This allows the weights to be used in the next iteration for computing the weighted error $\epsilon_{t+1}$ and for probability-based sampling if the algorithm is implemented via re-sampling rather than weighted loss.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for update rule, 1 mark for amplification/demotion logic, 1 mark for normalization rationale)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [14 Marks, KTU Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the AdaBoost algorithm in detail with the help of a labeled diagram. Derive the formula for the learner weight $\alpha_t$ by minimizing the exponential loss function. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a complete Python program using `scikit-learn` to train and evaluate an `AdaBoostClassifier` on the **Breast Cancer Wisconsin** dataset. Your program must:
   - Load the dataset
   - Split it into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets
   - Train an AdaBoostClassifier with 50 estimators
   - Compute and print the **accuracy**, **confusion matrix**, and **classification report** on the test set
   - Plot a **bar chart** of feature importances

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

**AdaBoost Algorithm (Schematic + Pseudocode):**

The AdaBoost algorithm proceeds in $T$ rounds. At each round, a weak learner is trained, evaluated via its weighted error, and assigned a trust weight $\alpha_t$. Sample weights are then updated so that the next learner focuses on the previously misclassified examples.

**Schematic Flow (3 marks):**

1. **Initialize** all sample weights equally: $w_i^{(1)} = 1/N$.
2. **For** $t = 1$ to $T$:
   - **Train** weak learner $h_t$ using the current weights $w^{(t)}$.
   - **Compute** weighted error: $\epsilon_t = \sum_{i: h_t(x_i) \neq y_i} w_i^{(t)}$.
   - **If** $\epsilon_t \ge 0.5$, **stop** (learner is no better than random).
   - **Compute** $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\left( \tfrac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t} \right)$.
   - **Update** weights: $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$.
   - **Normalize** weights so $\sum_i w_i^{(t+1)} = 1$.
3. **Output:** $H(x) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x) \right)$.

**Derivation of $\alpha_t$ via Exponential Loss Minimization (4 marks):**

Define the strong classifier after $t$ rounds as $F_t(x) = F_{t-1}(x) + \alpha_t h_t(x)$, where $F_0(x) = 0$. The exponential loss is:

$$L_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\big(-y_i F_t(x_i)\big) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i' \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)\big)$$

where $w_i' = \exp(-y_i F_{t-1}(x_i))$ is the implicit weight carried forward from previous rounds.

Splitting the sum over correctly classified ($y_i h_t(x_i) = +1$) and misclassified ($y_i h_t(x_i) = -1$) samples:

$$L_t = e^{-\alpha_t} \sum_{i \in C} w_i' + e^{\alpha_t} \sum_{i \in M} w_i' = (1 - \epsilon_t) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon_t e^{\alpha_t}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\alpha_t$:

$$\frac{dL_t}{d\alpha_t} = -(1 - \epsilon_t) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon_t e^{\alpha_t}$$

Setting to zero:

$$\epsilon_t e^{\alpha_t} = (1 - \epsilon_t) e^{-\alpha_t} \;\Longrightarrow\; e^{2\alpha_t} = \frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\boxed{\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t} \right)}$$

Substituting back confirms this is a **minimum** (second derivative is positive). This derivation shows that AdaBoost is a **greedy coordinate-descent optimization** of the exponential loss.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Python Implementation:**

```python
# ============================================================
# AdaBoost Classifier — Train & Evaluate on Breast Cancer Data
# ============================================================
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import AdaBoostClassifier
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report

# ---------- Step 1: Load the dataset ----------
data = load_breast_cancer()
X = pd.DataFrame(data.data, columns=data.feature_names)
y = pd.Series(data.target, name='target')
print("Dataset shape:", X.shape)
print("Classes:", np.unique(y))

# ---------- Step 2: Train/Test Split (80/20) ----------
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.20, random_state=42, stratify=y
)

# ---------- Step 3: Instantiate the AdaBoostClassifier ----------
base_stump = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1, random_state=42)
ada_model = AdaBoostClassifier(
    estimator=base_stump,
    n_estimators=50,
    learning_rate=1.0,
    random_state=42
)

# ---------- Step 4: Train the model ----------
ada_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# ---------- Step 5: Predict on test set ----------
y_pred = ada_model.predict(X_test)

# ---------- Step 6: Evaluate ----------
print("\n===== AdaBoost Classifier — Evaluation =====")
print("Test Accuracy :", accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred))
print("\nConfusion Matrix:\n", confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred))
print("\nClassification Report:\n", classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=data.target_names))

# ---------- Step 7: Feature Importance Bar Chart ----------
importances = ada_model.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)[::-1]

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.bar(range(X.shape[1]), importances[indices], color='steelblue', edgecolor='black')
plt.xticks(range(X.shape[1]), X.columns[indices], rotation=90)
plt.xlabel('Feature')
plt.ylabel('Importance')
plt.title('AdaBoost Classifier — Feature Importances (Breast Cancer Dataset)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Expected Output Highlights (approximate, for marking reference):**
- Test Accuracy ≈ **0.9649 – 0.9737** (very high, since the dataset is well-separated).
- Confusion Matrix will show very few misclassifications (typically 2–3 false negatives or false positives).
- Classification report will show **precision, recall, F1-score** all > 0.95.
- The feature importance chart will highlight the **top features** (typically `worst concave points`, `mean concave points`, `worst perimeter`).

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct dataset loading + split → 1 mark
- Correct AdaBoost instantiation with all key parameters → 2 marks
- `model.fit()` and `model.predict()` correctly used → 1 mark
- Accuracy, confusion matrix, classification report printed → 2 marks
- Feature importance bar chart code → 1 mark

---

### **Question B — [14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Bagging** and **Boosting**. Explain why AdaBoost is sensitive to **noisy data and outliers**, and propose one strategy to mitigate this issue. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a binary classification problem with 4 training samples having initial weights $w_i^{(1)} = 0.25$ each, suppose a weak learner $h_1$ misclassifies only sample 4. Calculate:
   - The weighted error $\epsilon_1$
   - The learner weight $\alpha_1$
   - The updated (unnormalized) weights $w_i^{(2)}$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$
   - The normalized weights $w_i^{(2)}$ after division by $Z_1$

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Bagging vs Boosting Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bagging (e.g., Random Forest) | Boosting (e.g., AdaBoost) |
|---|---|---|
| Training Strategy | Parallel; models trained independently | Sequential; each model depends on the previous |
| Sample Weighting | Uniform bootstrap sampling | Adaptive reweighting of misclassified samples |
| Main Goal | Reduce **Variance** | Reduce **Bias** |
| Weak Learner | Often deep trees | Often shallow stumps (depth 1) |
| Final Combination | Simple average / majority vote | Weighted sum with `sign` function |
| Sensitivity to Noise | Relatively robust | **Highly sensitive** |
| Example Algorithm | Random Forest, Bagged Trees | AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost |

**Why AdaBoost is Sensitive to Noisy Data and Outliers (3 marks):**

AdaBoost's weight-update mechanism aggressively amplifies the weights of misclassified samples. If a sample is a **label noise** (e.g., its true label was flipped incorrectly) or an **outlier** (it lies in a region of feature space that is unrepresentative of any class), it will be **consistently misclassified** across multiple boosting rounds. Each time it is misclassified, its weight is multiplied by $e^{\alpha_t} > 1$, causing its weight to grow **exponentially**.

In the final strong classifier, this single noisy/outlier sample can:
- Distort the decision boundary of subsequent weak learners.
- Force the algorithm to construct overly complex boundaries to fit a non-generalizable point.
- Lead to **severe overfitting**, where the training accuracy is near-perfect but test accuracy collapses.

**Mitigation Strategy (1 mark):**

Several strategies can mitigate this:
1. **Use a lower `learning_rate`** (e.g., $\nu = 0.1$ instead of 1.0) to dampen the exponential weight amplification.
2. **Use `RandomUnderSampler` or `SMOTE` preprocessing** to clean the training data before boosting.
3. **Switch to a more robust variant** like **Gentle Boost** or **Gradient Boosting with Huber loss**, which are less aggressive on outliers.
4. **Limit `n_estimators`** and use early stopping on a validation set to prevent the algorithm from chasing noise in later rounds.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $N = 4$, $w_i^{(1)} = 0.25$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$. Only sample 4 is misclassified by $h_1$.

**Step 1 — Weighted Error $\epsilon_1$ (2 marks):**

The weighted error is the sum of weights of misclassified samples. Only sample 4 is misclassified:

$$\epsilon_1 = w_4^{(1)} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Learner Weight $\alpha_1$ (2 marks):**

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - 0.25}{0.25} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left( \frac{0.75}{0.25} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(3) = \frac{1}{2}(1.0986) \approx 0.5493$$

**Step 3 — Updated Weights (Unnormalized) (2 marks):**

For correctly classified samples ($i = 1, 2, 3$): multiply weight by $e^{-\alpha_1} = e^{-0.5493} \approx 0.5774$

$$w_1^{(2)} = w_2^{(2)} = w_3^{(2)} = 0.25 \times 0.5774 \approx 0.1443$$

For misclassified sample ($i = 4$): multiply weight by $e^{\alpha_1} = e^{0.5493} \approx 1.7321$

$$w_4^{(2)} = 0.25 \times 1.7321 \approx 0.4330$$

**Step 4 — Normalized Weights (1 mark):**

The normalization constant is:

$$Z_1 = 3(0.1443) + 0.4330 = 0.4329 + 0.4330 = 0.8659$$

Normalized weights:

$$w_1^{(2)} = w_2^{(2)} = w_3^{(2)} = \frac{0.1443}{0.8659} \approx 0.1667$$

$$w_4^{(2)} = \frac{0.4330}{0.8659} \approx 0.5000$$

**Verification:** $3(0.1667) + 0.5000 = 0.5001 + 0.5000 \approx 1.0$ ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often compute the simple misclassification count (e.g., $1/4 = 0.25$) for the error, but for **boosting algorithms** the *weighted* error must be used. Even if the count fraction is the same initially, it diverges after the first update.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **normalize** the updated weights before passing them to the next iteration. If the sum of weights is not 1, the next $\epsilon_t$ will be incorrectly scaled.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Using $\ln$ (natural log) versus $\log_{10}$. The AdaBoost derivation uses the **natural logarithm** ($e$-based). Using base-10 logs is a fatal error.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (Python Lab):** Using `base_estimator` (deprecated in sklearn 1.2+) instead of `estimator`. Students writing exam answers should use the correct parameter name per the current sklearn API.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 (Python Lab):** Forgetting to set `random_state` for reproducibility, which causes the confusion matrix to differ between runs and may cost marks if the examiner expects deterministic output.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for AdaBoost:**

- ✅ AdaBoost is a **sequential ensemble** algorithm that combines weak learners into a strong learner.
- ✅ The base weak learner is typically a **decision stump** (`max_depth=1`).
- ✅ **Initialize** sample weights uniformly: $w_i^{(1)} = 1/N$.
- ✅ **Weighted error** $\epsilon_t = \sum_{i \in \text{misclassified}} w_i^{(t)}$.
- ✅ **Learner weight** $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\big(\tfrac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\big)$.
- ✅ **Sample weight update:** $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$.
- ✅ **Always normalize** weights after the update so $\sum w_i = 1$.
- ✅ **Stopping condition:** $\epsilon_t \ge 0.5$ (learner no better than random).
- ✅ **Final prediction:** $H(x) = \text{sign}\!\big(\sum_t \alpha_t h_t(x)\big)$.
- ✅ AdaBoost **minimizes the exponential loss** $L = \sum \exp(-yF(x))$.
- ✅ AdaBoost is **highly sensitive to noise and outliers** because of exponential weight amplification.
- ✅ `n_estimators` ($T$) and `learning_rate` ($\nu$) have an **inverse trade-off**.
- ✅ AdaBoost is **not natively parallelizable** (unlike Random Forest) — boosting is inherently sequential.
- ✅ For **multi-class** problems, sklearn uses the **SAMME** algorithm (Stagewise Additive Modeling using a Multi-class Exponential loss function).
- ✅ In sklearn ≥ 1.2, the parameter is `estimator=` (not the deprecated `base_estimator=`).
- ✅ The **margin maximization** property of AdaBoost is a key reason it often resists overfitting even after many rounds (until noise is encountered).
- ✅ AdaBoost is a **bias-reduction** technique primarily; it works best with high-bias, low-variance base learners.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reduce features using the Principal Components and Matrix factorization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL408 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module List of Experiments
> - **Topic:** Reduce features using the Principal Components and Matrix factorization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — Formal Definition

**Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a linear, unsupervised dimensionality-reduction technique that projects high-dimensional data onto a lower-dimensional subspace spanned by the directions (principal components) that maximize the variance of the projected data. Mathematically, PCA computes the **eigenvectors of the data covariance matrix** (or equivalently, the **right singular vectors of the centered data matrix**), and ranks them by their corresponding eigenvalues which quantify the amount of variance captured by each component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** PCA is an orthogonal linear transformation that converts the coordinates of a dataset into a new coordinate system such that the greatest variance lies along the first principal component (PC1), the second greatest along the second principal component (PC2), and so on.

### 1.2 Matrix Factorization (MF) — Formal Definition

**Matrix Factorization** refers to decomposing a (usually rectangular) data matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ into a product of two or more lower-rank factor matrices. In the context of feature reduction, the most common variant is **Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF)** where $\mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}$ with $\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{H} \geq 0$, and **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** where $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shadow Analogy for PCA:**
> Imagine a 3D statue of a person standing under a bright lamp on the floor. The **shadow cast on the 2D floor** is a lower-dimensional representation of the 3D object. PCA finds the **best angle** to place the lamp so that the resulting 2D shadow preserves the **maximum information (variance)** of the original 3D object. Just as different lamp angles produce different informative shadows, PCA finds the optimal projection axes (principal components) that retain the most meaningful structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Recipe Decomposition for Matrix Factorization:**
> Think of a **movie rating matrix** (users × movies) as a giant cookbook. Matrix factorization "discovers" hidden **latent features** (e.g., "action-loving users", "sci-fi movies") and decomposes the huge cookbook into two smaller cookbooks — one describing users by their taste, and another describing movies by their characteristics. The product reconstructs the original ratings.

### 1.4 Why Dimensionality Reduction?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Curse of Dimensionality:** As features grow, data becomes sparse, distance metrics lose meaning, and models overfit.
> - **Computational Efficiency:** Fewer features = faster training and inference.
> - **Visualization:** Reduce to 2D/3D for human interpretation.
> - **Noise Suppression:** Low-variance directions often encode noise.
> - **Storage:** Compressed representations require less memory.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics (PCA / MF)

- **Explained Variance Ratio:** $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_j \lambda_j}$
- **Reconstruction Error:** $\Vert \mathbf{X} - \hat{\mathbf{X}} \Vert_F^2$
- **Cumulative Explained Variance Threshold:** typically **0.95** for retaining components.
- **Frobenius Norm:** $\Vert \mathbf{X} \Vert_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i,j} x_{ij}^2}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps of PCA

PCA follows a deterministic, well-defined procedure:

1. **Center the Data:** Subtract the column-wise mean from every observation so that each feature has zero mean.
2. **Standardize (Optional):** Divide by the standard deviation to give all features unit variance — critical when features are on different scales (e.g., "age" vs "salary").
3. **Compute the Covariance Matrix:** $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$ (a symmetric $d \times d$ matrix).
4. **Eigendecomposition:** Solve $\mathbf{C} \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ to obtain eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ and eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_i$.
5. **Sort Components:** Order eigenvectors by descending eigenvalues (variance captured).
6. **Select Top-k Components:** Choose $k$ such that $\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i} \geq 0.95$.
7. **Project Data:** $\mathbf{X}_{\text{reduced}} = \mathbf{X}_{\text{centered}} \cdot \mathbf{W}_k$ where $\mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k]$.

### 2.2 Operational Steps of SVD-based Feature Reduction

1. **Center Data** $\mathbf{X}_{\text{centered}}$.
2. **Compute SVD:** $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$ where $\mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$, $\mathbf{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ (diagonal of singular values $\sigma_i$), $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.
3. **Truncate to Rank $k$:** Keep top-$k$ singular values and corresponding columns of $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$.
4. **Reconstruction:** $\mathbf{X}_{\text{approx}} = \mathbf{U}_k \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top}$.

### 2.3 Operational Steps of NMF

1. **Ensure non-negativity** of input data $\mathbf{X} \geq 0$.
2. **Initialize** $\mathbf{W} \geq 0$, $\mathbf{H} \geq 0$ randomly.
3. **Iteratively update** until convergence (multiplicative update rules):
   - $\mathbf{H} \leftarrow \mathbf{H} \odot \frac{\mathbf{W}^{\top} \mathbf{X}}{\mathbf{W}^{\top} \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}}$
   - $\mathbf{W} \leftarrow \mathbf{W} \odot \frac{\mathbf{X} \mathbf{H}^{\top}}{\mathbf{W} \mathbf{H} \mathbf{H}^{\top}}$
4. **Use columns of $\mathbf{H}$** as the reduced feature representation (latent features).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Centering | $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Subtract mean vector |
| Covariance Matrix | $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$ | $d \times d$ symmetric matrix |
| Eigen Equation | $\mathbf{C} \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | Defines PCs |
| Explained Variance (PC $i$) | $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Fraction of total variance |
| Reconstruction (PCA) | $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}_k \mathbf{W}_k^{\top} + \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Approximate data |
| SVD | $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$ | Full decomposition |
| Truncated SVD Rank-$k$ | $\mathbf{X}_k = \mathbf{U}_k \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top}$ | Best rank-$k$ approximation (Eckart-Young) |
| Reconstruction Error | $\Vert \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{X}_k \Vert_F^2 = \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$ | Sum of squared discarded singular values |
| NMF Decomposition | $\mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}$ with $\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{H} \geq 0$ | Additive parts-based |
| NMF Objective | $\min_{\mathbf{W},\mathbf{H}} \Vert \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{W}\mathbf{H} \Vert_F^2$ | Squared Frobenius loss |
| Singular-Value to Eigenvalue | $\lambda_i = \frac{\sigma_i^2}{n-1}$ | Relationship between SVD and PCA |

### 2.5 Why "The Why" Behind Each Step

- **Centering** is essential because PCA finds axes of *maximum variance* through the origin; without centering, the first PC would be biased toward the mean vector.
- **Standardization** is needed when features have different units; otherwise, the feature with the largest scale dominates the components.
- **Eigendecomposition** of the covariance matrix gives orthogonal directions of maximum variance.
- **SVD** on $\mathbf{X}$ directly gives the same right singular vectors as eigenvectors of $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$, but is numerically more stable.
- **Truncation** of singular values yields the *best low-rank approximation* in the Frobenius norm (Eckart-Young-Mirsky Theorem).
- **NMF** enforces non-negativity, producing additive, parts-based representations (e.g., face features as sums of eyes, nose, mouth).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Image Compression:** PCA and SVD compress images by keeping only the top-$k$ principal components (e.g., 50 components can reconstruct 95% of a face image).
> - **Face Recognition (Eigenfaces):** Turk & Pentland's classic use of PCA for biometric identification.
> - **Bioinformatics:** Reduce gene-expression data (thousands of genes) to a few principal components for cancer classification.
> - **Recommender Systems:** Matrix factorization (SVD, NMF) is the backbone of collaborative filtering at Netflix, Spotify, Amazon.
> - **NLP Topic Modeling:** NMF on term-document matrices uncovers latent topics.
> - **Anomaly Detection:** PCA reconstruction error flags outliers in industrial sensor data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Eigenvectors of the Covariance Matrix Maximize Variance

We want to find a unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ ($\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$) such that the variance of the projected data $\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}$ is maximized.

$$\text{Variance of projection} = \frac{1}{n-1} \Vert \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$$

We can write this as:

$$\frac{1}{n-1} (\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w})^{\top} (\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$$

The optimization problem is:

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}} \quad \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} = 1$$

Using the **Lagrange multiplier** $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} - 1)$$

Setting the partial derivative with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2 \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda \mathbf{w} = 0$$

This yields the **eigenvalue equation**:

$$\mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

Hence, the variance of the projection equals $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} = \lambda$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The maximum variance achievable along any direction equals the **largest eigenvalue** of $\mathbf{C}$, and that direction is the corresponding **eigenvector**. Repeating this in the orthogonal complement gives all subsequent principal components.

### 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence of PCA and SVD

Let the centered data matrix be $\mathbf{X}_c \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$. Its SVD is:

$$\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$$

where $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ contains the **right singular vectors**. The covariance matrix is:

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c$$

Substituting the SVD:

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1} (\mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top})^{\top} (\mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top})$$

Using $(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B})^{\top} = \mathbf{B}^{\top}\mathbf{A}^{\top}$:

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{V} \mathbf{\Sigma}^{\top} \mathbf{U}^{\top} \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$$

Since $\mathbf{U}$ is orthogonal, $\mathbf{U}^{\top} \mathbf{U} = \mathbf{I}$. Also $\mathbf{\Sigma}^{\top} \mathbf{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \ldots, \sigma_d^2)$:

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{V} \mathbf{\Sigma}^2 \mathbf{V}^{\top}$$

This is the **spectral decomposition** of $\mathbf{C}$, meaning $\mathbf{V}$ are the eigenvectors and $\lambda_i = \frac{\sigma_i^2}{n-1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** SVD is mathematically equivalent to PCA but is preferred in practice because (a) it does not require forming $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ (numerical stability), and (b) it gives $\mathbf{U}$ (low-dimensional coordinates) directly.

### 3.3 Derivation: Eckart-Young-Mirsky Theorem (Best Rank-k Approximation)

For a given $\mathbf{X}$ with SVD $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$, the best rank-$k$ approximation in the Frobenius norm is:

$$\mathbf{X}_k = \mathbf{U}_k \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top}$$

with squared error:

$$\Vert \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{X}_k \Vert_F^2 = \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$$

**Proof sketch:** Any rank-$k$ matrix can be written as $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{P} \mathbf{Q}^{\top}$ with $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$, $\mathbf{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$. The columns of $\mathbf{P}$ lie in the column space of $\mathbf{X}$ (it can be shown via projection that the optimal columns are in $\text{span}(\mathbf{U}_k)$). Then the error becomes:

$$\Vert \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{X}_k \Vert_F^2 = \Vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top} - \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{V}_k^{\top} \Vert_F^2$$

After algebra, this equals $\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$, the sum of squared discarded singular values.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (PCA on $2 \times 2$ data)

Consider the data matrix (already centered):

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{with } \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Centered data:

$$\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Covariance matrix:

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{4-1} \mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 20 \\ 20 & 20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.67 & 6.67 \\ 6.67 & 6.67 \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues: solve $\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$(6.67 - \lambda)^2 - 6.67^2 = 0 \implies \lambda^2 - 13.33\lambda = 0 \implies \lambda_1 = 13.33, \lambda_2 = 0$$

Eigenvector for $\lambda_1$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 6.67 & 6.67 \\ 6.67 & 6.67 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w_1 \\ w_2 \end{bmatrix} = 13.33 \begin{bmatrix} w_1 \\ w_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Solution: $w_1 = w_2$, normalized: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$.

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\lambda_2 = 0$, the data is perfectly collinear — we can reduce from 2D to 1D without information loss. The projected coordinates are $\mathbf{X}_{\text{reduced}} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{v}_1$.

### 3.5 Multiplicative Update Rules for NMF — Derivation Outline

The objective is $\min \Vert \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{W}\mathbf{H} \Vert_F^2$ with $\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{H} \geq 0$. Using the auxiliary function method (Lee & Seung, 2001), one derives the **multiplicative updates**:

$$\mathbf{H} \leftarrow \mathbf{H} \odot \frac{\mathbf{W}^{\top} \mathbf{X}}{\mathbf{W}^{\top} \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}}$$

$$\mathbf{W} \leftarrow \mathbf{W} \odot \frac{\mathbf{X} \mathbf{H}^{\top}}{\mathbf{W} \mathbf{H} \mathbf{H}^{\top}}$$

The Hadamard product $\odot$ and element-wise division guarantee non-negativity is preserved at every iteration. Convergence to a local minimum is guaranteed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PCA Pipeline — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Matrix X shape n x d] --> B[Step 1: Subtract Mean]
    B --> C[Step 2: Standardize if needed]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Covariance Matrix C]
    D --> E[Step 4: Eigendecomposition C v equals lambda v]
    E --> F[Step 5: Sort Eigenvalues Descending]
    F --> G[Step 6: Choose top k components]
    G --> H[Step 7: Project X to X reduced shape n x k]
    H --> I[Reduced Feature Set Ready for ML Model]
```

### 4.2 SVD-based Feature Reduction — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Centered Data Xc] --> B[SVD Computation U Sigma Vt]
    B --> C[Singular Values sigma1 sigma2 ... sigmad]
    B --> D[Right Singular Vectors V]
    C --> E{Keep top k singular values}
    D --> E
    E --> F[Truncated Uk Sigmak VkT]
    F --> G[Reduced Data Xreduced equals VkT or U k Sigmak]
    G --> H[Reconstruction Error equals sum of discarded sigma squared]
```

### 4.3 NMF Workflow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Non-negative Data Matrix X] --> B[Initialize W and H randomly W H greater or equal 0]
    B --> C[Update H using H times WtX over WtWH]
    C --> D[Update W using W times XHt over WHHt]
    D --> E{Convergence Check Frobenius norm diff less than epsilon}
    E -->|No| C
    E -->|Yes| F[Output H shape d x k as reduced features]
    F --> G[Optional: Reconstruction X approx equals WH]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Method Overview

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Dimensionality Reduction Techniques] --> P1[Linear Methods]
    M --> P2[Non-linear Methods]
    P1 --> PCA[Principal Component Analysis PCA]
    P1 --> SVD[Truncated SVD]
    P1 --> NMF[Non-negative Matrix Factorization]
    P1 --> LDA[Linear Discriminant Analysis]
    P2 --> TSNEt[t-SNE]
    P2 --> UMAP[UMAP]
    P2 --> KPCA[Kernel PCA]
    PCA --> PCAA[Unsupervised Linear Variance Max]
    SVD --> SVDA[Numerical Stable Eigen Equivalent]
    NMF --> NMFA[Parts Based Additive Non-negative]
```

### 4.5 Variance Retention Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Component Index 1 to d] --> B[Cumulative Explained Variance]
    B --> C[PC1 captures dominant variance]
    B --> D[PC2 captures next largest]
    B --> E[PC3 and beyond capture diminishing variance]
    C --> F[Threshold 0.95 line]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Select k components to cross threshold]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]** Define Principal Component Analysis. Why is it considered an unsupervised dimensionality reduction technique?

**Model Answer:**
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a statistical technique that transforms a $d$-dimensional dataset into a new $k$-dimensional coordinate system ($k \ll d$) by projecting the data onto orthogonal axes (principal components) ordered by the amount of variance they capture. It is **unsupervised** because it uses only the input features and does not require any class labels — the components are derived purely from the covariance structure of the data itself. (3 marks)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** Differentiate between PCA and Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) with respect to their constraints and output interpretation.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PCA | NMF |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Constraint on inputs | None (real-valued) | Non-negativity required |
| Decomposition | Eigendecomposition of covariance | $\mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{W}\mathbf{H}$ with $\mathbf{W},\mathbf{H} \geq 0$ |
| Interpretation | Variance-maximizing orthogonal axes | Additive, parts-based representation |
| Orthogonality | Components are orthogonal | Components are not necessarily orthogonal |

(3 marks)

---

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** What is the role of the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix in PCA?

**Model Answer:**
The eigenvalues of the covariance matrix quantify the **variance of the data along the corresponding eigenvector (principal component)**. The eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue is the direction of maximum variance (PC1), and so on. The sum of all eigenvalues equals the total variance of the dataset, which is why the **explained variance ratio** $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_j \lambda_j}$ is used to decide how many components to retain. (3 marks)

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]** State the Eckart-Young-Mirsky theorem. Mention its significance in matrix factorization.

**Model Answer:**
The Eckart-Young-Mirsky theorem states that the **best rank-$k$ approximation** of a matrix $\mathbf{X}$ (in the Frobenius or spectral norm) is obtained by truncating its SVD to the top $k$ singular values: $\mathbf{X}_k = \mathbf{U}_k \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top}$, and the squared error equals $\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$. It guarantees that no other rank-$k$ matrix can yield a smaller reconstruction error, making SVD the optimal dimensionality-reduction tool. (3 marks)

---

> **Q5. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]** Why is data standardization recommended before applying PCA?

**Model Answer:**
PCA seeks directions of maximum variance, so features with larger numeric scales (e.g., salary in lakhs vs age in years) would dominate the principal components. **Standardization** (subtract mean, divide by standard deviation) places all features on a comparable scale, ensuring that the resulting components reflect the underlying correlation structure of the data rather than the arbitrary units of measurement. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] With a suitable mathematical derivation, explain the working of Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Clearly mention the role of covariance matrix, eigenvalues, and eigenvectors.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] For the following centered data matrix, compute the principal components and the variance explained by each:
>
> $$\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 3 \\ 5 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Reduce it to 1-D and write the projected coordinates.

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Mathematical Derivation of PCA

**Step 1: Problem Setup**
Given centered data $\mathbf{X}_c \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, find a unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes the variance of the projection.

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}} \; \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} = 1$$

**Step 2: Lagrangian Formulation**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} - 1)$$

**Step 3: Differentiate and set to zero**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2 \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda \mathbf{w} = 0$$

**Step 4: Eigenvalue equation**

$$\mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 5: Conclusion**
The maximum variance is the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$ of $\mathbf{C}$, and the corresponding **eigenvector** $\mathbf{w}_1$ is the first principal component. Subsequent PCs are the eigenvectors corresponding to the next-largest eigenvalues, automatically orthogonal due to the symmetry of $\mathbf{C}$. (7 marks — including statement of algorithm, derivation, and interpretation)

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1: Compute Covariance Matrix**

$$\mathbf{X}_c^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{X}_c^{\top} \mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 4+9+16+25 & 2+6+12+20 \\ 2+6+12+20 & 1+4+9+16 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 54 & 40 \\ 40 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{4-1} \begin{bmatrix} 54 & 40 \\ 40 & 30 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 & 13.33 \\ 13.33 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Eigendecomposition**

Characteristic equation:

$$\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (18 - \lambda)(10 - \lambda) - 13.33^2 = 0$$

$$180 - 18\lambda - 10\lambda + \lambda^2 - 177.69 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - 28\lambda + 2.31 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{28 \pm \sqrt{784 - 9.24}}{2} = \frac{28 \pm \sqrt{774.76}}{2} = \frac{28 \pm 27.83}{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{55.83}{2} = 27.92, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{0.17}{2} = 0.083$$

**Step 3: Compute PC1 Eigenvector**

For $\lambda_1 = 27.92$:

$$(18 - 27.92)w_1 + 13.33 w_2 = 0 \implies -9.92 w_1 + 13.33 w_2 = 0 \implies w_2 = 0.744 w_1$$

Normalize: $\sqrt{w_1^2 + (0.744 w_1)^2} = 1 \implies w_1 \sqrt{1.554} = 1 \implies w_1 = 0.802$.

$$\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.802 \\ 0.597 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Variance Explained**

$$\text{EVR}_1 = \frac{27.92}{27.92 + 0.083} = 0.997 \quad (99.7\%), \quad \text{EVR}_2 = 0.003 \; (0.3\%)$$

**Step 5: Project to 1-D**

$$\mathbf{X}_{\text{reduced}} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0.802) + 1(0.597) \\ 3(0.802) + 2(0.597) \\ 4(0.802) + 3(0.597) \\ 5(0.802) + 4(0.597) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.201 \\ 3.600 \\ 4.999 \\ 6.398 \end{bmatrix}$$

(7 marks)

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Alternative]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and demonstrate with a worked example how truncated SVD can be used to reduce the dimensionality of a dataset.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) with its update rules. Compare NMF with PCA in terms of interpretation and constraints. State two real-world applications of NMF.

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — SVD and Truncation

**Definition:** For any matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ with rank $r$, the SVD is:

$$\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ — left singular vectors (orthogonal)
- $\mathbf{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ — diagonal matrix of singular values $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \ldots \geq \sigma_r > 0$
- $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — right singular vectors (orthogonal)

**Truncation to Rank $k$:** Keep only the top $k$ singular values and the first $k$ columns of $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$:

$$\mathbf{X}_k = \mathbf{U}_k \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top}$$

This yields the **best rank-$k$ approximation** in the Frobenius norm (Eckart-Young-Mirsky).

**Worked Example:**
Consider:

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 6 \\ 6 & 10 \end{bmatrix}$ are $16$ and $4$, so singular values are $\sigma_1 = 4$, $\sigma_2 = 2$.

Right singular vectors: eigenvectors of $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$:
- For $\lambda = 16$: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, 1]^{\top}$
- For $\lambda = 4$: $\mathbf{v}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, -1]^{\top}$

Left singular vectors: $\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} \mathbf{X} \mathbf{v}_i$:
- $\mathbf{u}_1 = \frac{1}{4} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- $\mathbf{u}_2 = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$

SVD:

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Rank-1 Truncation:** $\mathbf{X}_1 = 4 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$

The reduced representation of any column $\mathbf{x}_i$ is $\mathbf{V}_k^{\top} \mathbf{x}_i$ (projection onto top $k$ right singular vectors). (7 marks)

---

#### Part (b) — NMF Explanation and Comparison

**NMF Concept:**
Decompose a non-negative matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^{m \times n}$ into $\mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}$ with $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^{m \times k}$ and $\mathbf{H} \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}^{k \times n}$, where $k \ll \min(m, n)$.

**Objective Function:**

$$\min_{\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{H}} \; \Vert \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{W}\mathbf{H} \Vert_F^2$$

**Multiplicative Update Rules (Lee-Seung):**

$$\mathbf{H} \leftarrow \mathbf{H} \odot \frac{\mathbf{W}^{\top} \mathbf{X}}{\mathbf{W}^{\top} \mathbf{W} \mathbf{H}}$$

$$\mathbf{W} \leftarrow \mathbf{W} \odot \frac{\mathbf{X} \mathbf{H}^{\top}}{\mathbf{W} \mathbf{H} \mathbf{H}^{\top}}$$

**Comparison with PCA:**

| Aspect | PCA | NMF |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Constraint | Real-valued | Non-negativity of factors |
| Output meaning | Variance-maximizing orthogonal axes | Additive, parts-based |
| Reconstruction | Orthogonal projection | Approximate, iterative |
| Best for | Continuous data, any sign | Image, text, counts (always non-negative) |
| Sparsity | Dense | Naturally sparse factors |

**Real-world Applications of NMF:**

1. **Face Recognition Parts Decomposition:** NMF decomposes a face image database into parts (eyes, nose, mouth) and their spatial locations, useful for robust face recognition.
2. **Topic Modeling in NLP:** NMF on a term-document (TF-IDF) matrix extracts latent topics — each column of $\mathbf{W}$ is a topic, each row of $\mathbf{H}$ is the topic distribution of a document.

(7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Skipping Data Centering:** Students often apply eigendecomposition directly to $\mathbf{X}$ instead of $\mathbf{X}_c$ — this gives wrong eigenvectors and PC1 biases toward the mean direction. (Lose 2-3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to Normalize Eigenvectors:** Principal components must be unit vectors. Many students report unnormalized eigenvectors and lose marks on the projection step. (Lose 1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing Singular Values with Eigenvalues:** Singular values of $\mathbf{X}$ and eigenvalues of $\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$ are related by $\lambda = \sigma^2$. Students mixing these will get wrong answers.
> 4. **Not Checking Non-negativity in NMF:** The hallmark of NMF is the non-negativity constraint. Students who apply standard SVD-style factorization without enforcing $\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{H} \geq 0$ are doing PCA, not NMF.
> 5. **Explained Variance Calculation Errors:** The denominator must be $\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i$ (sum of all eigenvalues), not just the top $k$. Cumulative variance across all components equals 1.
> 6. **Misinterpreting Reconstruction Error:** $\Vert \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{X}_k \Vert_F^2 = \sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$ is the discarded variance, not the retained variance.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **PCA** is **unsupervised**, **linear**, and **variance-maximizing**.
- ✅ Always **center** (and **standardize** when features differ in scale) before PCA.
- ✅ The **eigenvectors of the covariance matrix** are the principal components.
- ✅ The **eigenvalues** measure variance along each principal component.
- ✅ **Explained Variance Ratio** = $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j} \lambda_j}$; choose $k$ such that cumulative EVR $\geq$ **0.95**.
- ✅ **SVD** $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$ is equivalent to PCA but more numerically stable; $\mathbf{V}$ columns = PCs.
- ✅ **Truncated SVD** gives the **best rank-$k$ approximation** (Eckart-Young-Mirsky).
- ✅ **Reconstruction Error** from truncation = $\sum_{i=k+1}^{r} \sigma_i^2$.
- ✅ **NMF** = $\mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{W}\mathbf{H}$ with $\mathbf{W}, \mathbf{H} \geq 0$ — **parts-based**, additive.
- ✅ **NMF updates** preserve non-negativity (multiplicative rules, Lee & Seung).
- ✅ PCA produces **orthogonal** axes; NMF components are generally **not orthogonal**.
- ✅ Use **PCA** for general continuous data; use **NMF** for non-negative data (images, counts, TF-IDF).
- ✅ **Scree plot** (eigenvalue vs component index) and **cumulative variance plot** are the standard diagnostic tools to choose $k$.
- ✅ PCA is **sensitive to outliers** — consider **Robust PCA** or **preprocessing** (e.g., winsorization) for noisy industrial data.
- ✅ **Kernel PCA** and **t-SNE** are non-linear alternatives when data lies on a curved manifold.
- ✅ Common KTU exam keywords: "centered data", "covariance matrix", "eigenvalue equation", "cumulative explained variance", "truncated SVD", "Frobenius norm", "non-negativity constraint".
- ✅ Standard implementation order: **center → standardize → covariance → eigendecompose → sort → project**.
- ✅ For **images** (eigenfaces), each row of $\mathbf{X}$ is a flattened image and eigenvectors reshape back to "eigenface" images.
- ✅ For **recommender systems**, $\mathbf{X}$ is the user-item rating matrix; matrix factorization reveals latent user/item features.
- ✅ KTU lab viva question: *"What happens if you do NOT center the data before PCA?"* — Answer: PC1 aligns with the mean vector, not the direction of maximum variance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement robot path plan using BFS algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL408 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module List of Experiments
> - **Topic:** Implement robot path plan using BFS algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Robot Path Planning** is the computational problem of finding a continuous, collision-free trajectory for a robot from a designated **start configuration** to a **goal configuration** within a workspace containing obstacles. In the discrete grid-based formulation adopted by the KTU PCRAL408 lab, the workspace is abstracted as a 2-D occupancy grid $\mathcal{G} \in \{0, 1\}^{m \times n}$, where cell value **0** denotes *free space* (traversable) and **1** denotes *occupied space* (obstacle). The **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** algorithm solves this planning problem by systematically expanding nodes in non-decreasing order of path cost from the start, guaranteeing the **shortest path** in terms of number of moves on any unweighted graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Lab Manual):** *"Breadth-First Search is an uninformed graph traversal algorithm that explores all vertices at the present depth before moving to vertices at the next depth level. It uses a FIFO queue and is optimal for finding the shortest path on unweighted grids."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ripples in a Pond"

Imagine dropping a **pebble** into the centre of a still pond. The ripples do **not** jump to the far corner in one leap; instead, they expand outward in **perfect concentric circles**. The first ring touches cells that are *1 step* away, the second ring touches cells *2 steps* away, and so on. BFS behaves identically:

- The **pebble** = your robot's starting position $S$.
- The **water surface** = the navigable grid.
- The **ripples** = successive frontier layers of exploration.
- A **stone (rock) in the pond** = an obstacle cell.
- The **first ripple that touches the goal** $G$ = the shortest guaranteed route.

Because BFS visits every cell at distance $d$ **before** any cell at distance $d+1$, the moment it discovers the goal, that discovery path is provably optimal in move-count.

### 1.3 Robot Path Planning vs. General Graph Search

| Property | General Graph Search | Grid Robot Path Planning |
|----------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Nodes | Abstract vertices | Grid cells $(r, c)$ |
| Edges | User-defined adjacency | 4-neighbour or 8-neighbour moves |
| Edge weight | May be weighted | Uniform weight = 1 |
| Heuristic required | For A*, GBFS | **Not required** for BFS (uninformed) |
| Optimality | Not always guaranteed | **Always shortest path** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCRAL408 Module 1):** BFS-based path planning is the canonical introductory experiment used to teach the student the bridge between *classical AI search* and *real-world robotics*. Mastering this experiment is mandatory before progressing to informed search (A*), dynamic programming (Dijkstra), and learning-based planners (RRT*, RL).

### 1.4 Physical / Computational Constants Used

- **Neighbourhood constant $k$**: $k = 4$ (Von Neumann) or $k = 8$ (Moore).
- **Queue access time**: $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized for `collections.deque` in Python.
- **Grid cardinality**: For an $m \times n$ grid, $|\mathcal{V}| = m \cdot n$ nodes and $|\mathcal{E}|_{\max} = 2mn$ undirected edges (4-connected case).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Decomposition — The Six Logical Steps

1. **Grid Encoding.** Read the workspace into a 2-D matrix $\mathcal{G}[m][n]$. Mark *start* cell $S = (r_s, c_s)$ and *goal* cell $G = (r_g, c_g)$. Cell value semantics:
   $$\mathcal{G}[i][j] = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{free cell (traversable)} \\ 1 & \text{obstacle (blocked)} \end{cases}$$

2. **State Representation.** Each state is a tuple $s = (r, c) \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}^2$ identifying a unique grid coordinate. The state-space cardinality is $|\mathcal{S}| = m \cdot n$.

3. **Successor Function.** From any non-boundary, non-obstacle cell, generate valid neighbours. For the **4-connected (Von Neumann)** action set $\mathcal{A}_4$:
   $$\mathcal{A}_4 = \{(-1, 0),\ (+1, 0),\ (0, -1),\ (0, +1)\}$$
   Each move has uniform cost $c(s, s') = 1$.

4. **Frontier Management (FIFO Queue).** Initialise queue $\mathcal{Q} \leftarrow \text{deque}([S])$. Dequeue the *oldest* element first — this is what enforces the breadth-first, layer-by-layer expansion.

5. **Visited / Parent Tracking.** Maintain two auxiliary structures:
   - $\text{visited}[m][n]$ — boolean matrix preventing revisits.
   - $\text{parent}[m][n]$ — stores the predecessor cell $(r', c')$ from which each visited cell was reached.

6. **Goal Test & Path Reconstruction.** When $G$ is dequeued (or discovered), terminate search. Backtrack from $G$ to $S$ via the `parent` pointers to obtain the optimal path:
   $$P^* = \text{backtrack}(G) = \{(r_g, c_g),\ \text{parent}(G),\ \text{parent}^2(G),\ \dots,\ S\}$$

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a Queue and not a Stack?** A stack (DFS) dives deep along one axis; it may find *a* path but **not** the shortest. The queue's FIFO nature guarantees *level-order* expansion, which is mathematically equivalent to Dijkstra's algorithm when all edge costs are 1.
- **Why a `parent` map?** Without it, we would know the goal is reachable but not the route. The `parent` matrix encodes a *search tree* rooted at $S$.
- **Why the `visited` set?** A grid is a **graph with cycles** (you can return to a previous cell). Without `visited`, BFS would loop forever and lose optimality guarantees.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of vertices (4-conn.) | $V = m \cdot n$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Maximum undirected edges (4-conn.) | $E_{\max} = 2mn - m - n$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Maximum undirected edges (8-conn.) | $E_{\max} = 4mn - 3m - 3n + 2$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | BFS time complexity | $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ | per call |
| 5 | BFS space complexity | $\mathcal{O}(V)$ | visited + queue + parent |
| 6 | Path optimality (unweighted) | Guaranteed — *shortest in moves* | moves, not Euclidean |
| 7 | Enqueue / Dequeue cost | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | using `collections.deque` |
| 8 | Branching factor $b$ (4-conn.) | $b \leq 4$ | interior cells |
| 9 | Branching factor $b$ (8-conn.) | $b \leq 8$ | interior cells |
| 10 | Effective depth $d$ (Manhattan) | $d = \vert r_g - r_s \vert + \vert c_g - c_s \vert$ | in cells |
| 11 | Effective depth $d$ (Chebyshev) | $d = \max(\vert r_g - r_s \vert,\ \vert c_g - c_s \vert)$ | in cells |
| 12 | Reachability test | $\exists$ path iff $G \in \text{visited}$ on termination | Boolean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical bars in tables use `\vert`:** Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE encoding rules, expressions like $\vert x \vert$ are written using `\vert` so they do not collide with markdown pipe `|` separators.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

BFS path planning underpins **warehouse robotics (Amazon Kiva)**, **automated vacuum cleaners (Roomba — early generations)**, **chip-design routing**, **maze-solving micro-mouse competitions**, and the **GPS layer** of in-car navigation for uniformly-weighted urban grids. It is also a foundational subroutine in **BFS-based bi-directional search**, **0-1 BFS**, and **multi-source BFS** (e.g., fire-evacuation simulation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Formulation of BFS Optimality
